title: The ethics of photographing strangers in religious spaces
summary: Argue whether visitors should be allowed to photograph worshippers inside mosques, temples, and churches — or whether the camera changes the place too much.
topic: Whether tourist photography inside active places of worship is acceptable
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether tourists should be allowed to photograph people who are praying or worshipping inside active religious buildings (mosques, temples, churches, gurdwaras), or whether worshippers deserve the right to pray without becoming background subjects in someone else's holiday album. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in at least one named city or building. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a phone camera quietly does to a space designed for stillness is something most visitors never…
Admittedly, the architectural beauty of major religious buildings is part of why tourists visit at all, and a sweeping ban on photography would…

Standing in the courtyard of the Süleymaniye Mosque in Istanbul on a Friday afternoon, one sees, in the same frame, an elderly man prostrating in prayer and a visitor in shorts photographing him from…

hintVocab.meaningVi: không gian tôn giáo đang hoạt động
động tác phủ phục trong lễ nghi
xâm phạm quyền riêng tư của ai đó
sự đồng ý có hiểu biết
lệnh cấm bao trùm / toàn diện

hintVocab.phrase: to be reduced to an aesthetic
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị thu nhỏ thành chỉ một yếu tố thẩm mỹ
làm gián đoạn sự chiêm nghiệm
âm thanh chụp ảnh giảm âm
khu vực được phép chụp ảnh
hỏi trực tiếp người cầu nguyện

sampleText:
Inside an active mosque on a Friday afternoon, the same physical space holds two profoundly different kinds of activity. A man kneeling in prayer is, in that moment, doing something he believes is between himself and God. A visitor standing six metres away, lifting a phone, is doing something quite different: composing a frame. The question is whether the second activity has any business taking place over the first.

What a phone camera quietly does to a space designed for stillness is something most visitors never have to confront. Worshippers, photographed without consent in the middle of an act of devotion, are being converted — in real time, in their own holy place — into the visual content of someone else's afternoon. The exchange is not equal. The photographer takes home an image; the photographed person takes home the experience of having been seen, and recorded, while genuinely vulnerable.

Admittedly, the architectural beauty of major religious buildings is part of why tourists visit at all, and a sweeping ban on photography would impoverish the way these places are remembered and shared. The Hagia Sophia, Angkor Wat, the Blue Mosque, the Golden Temple — none of these would be as widely loved without the millions of images that have circulated of them. The buildings themselves, in some real sense, were made to be looked at.

Even so, the buildings are not the issue. The issue is the people inside them. What a reasonable policy looks like, in practice, is something narrower than either a free-for-all or a ban: free photography of empty spaces, sacred geometry, ornament, and architectural detail; explicit restriction on photographing identifiable individuals at prayer without permission. Several active mosques in Istanbul, including the Süleymaniye, already operate this way through clear signage and gentle enforcement by attendants. The system is not perfect, but it draws the right distinction.

Granted, asking permission of every person in a frame is impractical in busy buildings, and any reasonable rule has to accept some grey area at the edges. Wide architectural shots that incidentally include silhouettes at the back of a hall are not the same as a close-up of an elderly woman's face during her prayer. The right test is not whether the photograph contains a person, but whether the person is recognisable and is the subject.

Religious buildings will continue to draw cameras. The question is whether the worshippers inside them are treated as fellow occupants of the space or as scenic background. Treated well, they remain people. Treated as content, they become a kind of unpaid extra in a holiday no one invited them to.

làm gián đoạn sự chiêm nghiệm

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to focus the reader on a usually invisible effect
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a phone camera quietly does to a space designed for stillness is something most visitors never have to confront' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để buộc người đọc nhìn vào một hậu quả mà bình thường họ sẽ bỏ qua. Trạng từ 'quietly' giúp đối lập âm thầm hành động của máy ảnh với tính chất tĩnh lặng của không gian.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a phone camera quietly does to a space designed for stillness is something most visitors never have to confront' uses a what-cleft to force the reader to look at a consequence they normally overlook. The adverb 'quietly' sets the camera's intrusion in subtle opposition to the stillness of the room.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Continuous passive with parenthetical insertion for moral emphasis
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Worshippers, photographed without consent in the middle of an act of devotion, are being converted — in real time, in their own holy place — into the visual content of someone else's afternoon' dùng dạng bị động tiếp diễn ('are being converted') để diễn tả quá trình đang xảy ra, và chèn vào giữa hai cụm trạng từ giữa dấu gạch ngang để tăng cường tính khẩn cấp đạo đức. Cấu trúc này nén toàn bộ luận điểm chính của bài vào một câu duy nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Worshippers, photographed without consent in the middle of an act of devotion, are being converted — in real time, in their own holy place — into the visual content of someone else's afternoon' uses the continuous passive ('are being converted') to describe an ongoing process, and inserts two adverbial phrases between dashes to intensify moral urgency. The structure compresses the essay's core argument into a single sentence.

sampleTranslationVi:
Bên trong một nhà thờ Hồi giáo đang hoạt động vào một chiều thứ Sáu, cùng một không gian vật lý chứa đựng hai loại hoạt động hoàn toàn khác nhau. Một người đàn ông quỳ gối cầu nguyện, trong khoảnh khắc đó, đang làm điều mà ông tin là giữa bản thân và Chúa. Một du khách đứng cách đó sáu mét, nâng điện thoại lên, đang làm điều gì đó khá khác: đang sắp xếp một khung hình. Câu hỏi là liệu hoạt động thứ hai có quyền diễn ra chồng lên hoạt động thứ nhất hay không.

Điều mà một chiếc máy ảnh điện thoại âm thầm làm với một không gian được thiết kế cho sự tĩnh lặng là điều mà hầu hết khách tham quan không bao giờ phải đối mặt. Những người cầu nguyện, bị chụp ảnh không có sự đồng ý giữa một hành động sùng kính, đang bị chuyển hóa — trong thời gian thực, trong chính nơi thiêng liêng của họ — thành nội dung trực quan của buổi chiều của người khác. Sự trao đổi không bình đẳng. Người chụp ảnh mang về nhà một bức ảnh; người bị chụp ảnh mang về nhà trải nghiệm về việc đã bị nhìn thấy, và ghi lại, trong khi đang thực sự dễ tổn thương.

Thừa nhận rằng, vẻ đẹp kiến trúc của các tòa nhà tôn giáo lớn là một phần lý do tại sao du khách đến tham quan, và một lệnh cấm chụp ảnh bao trùm sẽ làm nghèo nàn cách những nơi này được ghi nhớ và chia sẻ. Hagia Sophia, Angkor Wat, Nhà thờ Hồi giáo Xanh, Đền Vàng — không nơi nào trong số này được yêu mến rộng rãi như vậy nếu không có hàng triệu hình ảnh đã được lưu truyền về chúng. Bản thân các tòa nhà, theo một nghĩa thực sự nào đó, được tạo ra để được nhìn ngắm.

Dẫu vậy, các tòa nhà không phải là vấn đề. Vấn đề là những người bên trong chúng. Một chính sách hợp lý trông như thế nào, trong thực tế, là một điều gì đó hẹp hơn cả việc cho phép tự do hoàn toàn lẫn việc cấm hoàn toàn: tự do chụp ảnh các không gian trống, hình học thiêng liêng, hoa văn, và chi tiết kiến trúc; hạn chế rõ ràng đối với việc chụp ảnh các cá nhân có thể nhận dạng được đang cầu nguyện mà không có sự cho phép. Một số nhà thờ Hồi giáo đang hoạt động ở Istanbul, bao gồm cả Süleymaniye, đã vận hành theo cách này thông qua biển báo rõ ràng và sự thực thi nhẹ nhàng của các nhân viên. Hệ thống không hoàn hảo, nhưng nó vạch ra sự phân biệt đúng đắn.

Thừa nhận rằng, xin phép từng người trong khung hình là không thực tế trong các tòa nhà đông đúc, và bất kỳ quy tắc hợp lý nào cũng phải chấp nhận một vùng xám ở các rìa. Những bức ảnh kiến trúc rộng tình cờ bao gồm các bóng dáng ở cuối hành lang không giống với một bức cận cảnh khuôn mặt của một bà cụ trong khi cầu nguyện. Bài kiểm tra đúng không phải là liệu bức ảnh có chứa một người hay không, mà là liệu người đó có thể nhận dạng được và có phải là chủ thể hay không.

Các tòa nhà tôn giáo sẽ tiếp tục thu hút máy ảnh. Câu hỏi là liệu những người cầu nguyện bên trong chúng có được đối xử như những người cùng sử dụng không gian hay như phông nền cảnh quan. Được đối xử tốt, họ vẫn là con người. Được đối xử như nội dung, họ trở thành một kiểu diễn viên phụ không lương trong kỳ nghỉ mà không ai mời họ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to highlight a hidden effect of the camera?
options: Most visitors what a phone camera does quietly never have to confront a space stillness designed for is.
What a phone camera quietly does to a space designed for stillness is something most visitors never have to confront.
A space designed for stillness what a camera does is most visitors confront never quietly something.
Confront most visitors never what something is to a space designed for stillness a phone camera quietly does.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' delays the evaluation to the end of the sentence and forces the reader to attend to what they normally miss.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the new content delayed to the end.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'informed consent' mean in this context?
options: Permission given by a worshipper who genuinely understands what is being asked and agrees to it
A signed legal document required before entering a religious building
An announcement made over a loudspeaker informing worshippers of camera use
A general permit from the building's owner allowing photography
mcQuestions.explanation: Informed consent is permission given by a person who has been told clearly what they are agreeing to — it is the standard for ethical photography of identifiable individuals.
mcQuestions.hint: Break the phrase apart: 'informed' (having been told) + 'consent' (agreement).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'admittedly' correctly as a concession?
options: Admittedly, the architectural beauty of major religious buildings is part of why tourists visit at all.
Architectural beauty admittedly tourists visit major religious buildings of why is part at all.
Tourists at all admittedly visit major buildings religious of why architectural beauty is part.
Visit at all admittedly tourists why of part architectural beauty is religious buildings major.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly,' followed by a complete clause grants a real point to the opposing view before the essay continues its argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Admittedly,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a continuous passive to stress an ongoing intentional process?
options: Worshippers are being converted into the visual content of someone else's afternoon.
Worshippers convert into someone else's visual content afternoon being.
Into someone's visual content the worshippers being converted are afternoon.
Afternoon being content someone visual into worshippers converted are.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'are being converted' is the continuous passive, stressing that this conversion is happening in real time, repeatedly, as the worshipper prays.
mcQuestions.hint: Continuous passive looks like 'are/is being + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'background subject' mean in the essay?
options: The main person being photographed
A person who appears incidentally in a photograph composed around something else
A subject studied in school as additional reading
A piece of music played quietly behind the main melody
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's worry is that worshippers are being reduced to figures in the background of someone else's image — visible but not chosen or consulted.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'background' versus 'foreground' in a photograph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'the exchange is not equal'?
options: The two parties traded goods of different financial value.
What each side gains and loses from the interaction is profoundly different in weight.
The photographer paid less than the worshipper would have liked.
The worshipper agreed to be photographed but later changed their mind.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay sets up a contrast: the photographer keeps a casual image, while the photographed person carries an experience of intrusion. The 'exchange' (the implicit deal between them) is therefore unequal in moral weight.
mcQuestions.hint: What does each side actually gain and lose from this interaction?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'sacred geometry' mean in this essay?
options: Mathematical calculations performed in religious schools
The intentional geometric patterns and proportions used in religious art and architecture
A type of yoga performed in temples
The legal boundaries of religious property
mcQuestions.explanation: Sacred geometry refers to the patterns and proportions deliberately built into religious art and architecture — domes, arches, tilework — that carry symbolic and spiritual meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'sacred' + 'geometry' — what kind of patterns might that produce in a mosque or temple?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'unpaid extra in a holiday no one invited them to'?
options: A worshipper who is treated as background performer in a tourist's experience without being asked or compensated
A volunteer at a religious festival who serves visitors for free
A tour guide who is not paid by the religious building's management
A holidaymaker whose flight is not covered by their travel insurance
mcQuestions.explanation: The metaphor casts the worshipper as a film 'extra' — a person who appears in a scene but has no script, no role, no compensation, and no choice — in a holiday that belongs to someone else.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'extra' as a background actor in a film — what does that comparison imply?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the buildings are not the issue.
On the contrary, no building matters at all.
Furthermore, all buildings should be photographed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the concession (the architecture matters and should not be banned) and pivots back to the main argument that the people, not the buildings, are the ethical concern.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and refocuses on the real issue?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All photography inside religious buildings should be banned.
Tourists should be free to photograph whatever they like inside religious buildings.
Photography of architecture and empty spaces is fine; photographing identifiable individuals at prayer without permission is not.
Only journalists with press credentials should be allowed to photograph inside religious buildings.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues for a clear middle position: architecture and spaces are fair game; identifiable, recognisable people at prayer are not without consent.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the fourth paragraph for the writer's clearest statement of policy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly snapping photos of people praying is just kind of gross and obviously wrong.
Tourists are basically all over religious sites with their phones and it's super annoying.
Photographing identifiable individuals during private religious practice without informed consent constitutes a meaningful breach of personal dignity.
People who take pictures of others praying are pretty awful, full stop.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('identifiable individuals', 'informed consent', 'meaningful breach of personal dignity') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal hedges.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'basically', 'kind of', 'pretty', and other colloquial markers.

options: A specific area officially marked as a place where photography is allowed
A photography contest organised by a religious building
A studio attached to a religious building where formal portraits are taken
A digital area on a smartphone screen used for editing photographs
mcQuestions.explanation: A designated photography zone is a marked area where photography is permitted, while other parts of the building may be off-limits to cameras.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'designated' as 'officially marked' — for what purpose?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: Wide architectural shots that incidentally include silhouettes at the back of a hall are not the same as a close-up of an elderly woman's face during her prayer.
That incidentally include silhouettes wide architectural shots at the back of a hall not the same are.
Close-up of an elderly woman's face shots wide architectural include silhouettes hall back at the during her prayer not the same.
Are not the same shots wide that close-up architectural elderly woman face silhouettes back.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'that incidentally include silhouettes at the back of a hall' is a defining relative clause specifying which architectural shots the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'that', 'which', or 'who'.

options: To turn off the lights in a room used for meditation
To break the quiet, inward focus required for prayer or reflection
To rearrange the seating in a religious building
To interrupt a class about religious philosophy
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Contemplation' is a quiet, inward focus, often religious or meditative; to disrupt it is to break that focus, which a sudden camera flash or movement can do.
mcQuestions.hint: What kind of mental state is 'contemplation', and what would interrupt it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay with a sharp distinction?
options: In conclusion, photography and worship can coexist if we are careful.
Therefore, all cameras should be banned from religious buildings.
Treated well, they remain people. Treated as content, they become a kind of unpaid extra in a holiday no one invited them to.
To sum up, both photographers and worshippers matter in religious buildings.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C closes with a sharp parallel ('Treated well … Treated as content …') that crystallises the essay's central distinction in two memorable sentences.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a memorable parallel construction rather than a vague summary?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the right test' refer to in the essay's policy proposal?
options: A formal exam that tourists must pass before entering a religious building
The criterion the essay proposes for deciding whether a photograph is acceptable — whether a person is recognisable and is the subject
A scientific test that measures the intrusiveness of a camera flash
An exam given to professional photographers seeking access to religious sites
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay proposes a specific decision rule: ignore whether a person is technically in the frame, and ask instead whether they are recognisable and central to the shot.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five, which states 'the right test'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever been photographed without your permission in a moment when you would have preferred not to be? If so, what did it feel like — and how does that experience shape your view of tourists photographing worshippers in active religious spaces?
